title: Climate change, bank liquidity and systemic risk
summary: This ECB Working Paper uses transaction-level repo market data from 2019-2022 to examine how banks' exposure to climate transition risk affects borrowing costs. The study finds that banks with higher financed emissions pay 7-12% higher repo rates, driven by both a credit risk premium and an "inconvenience premium" reflecting dealers' sustainability preferences. The carbon premium triples during financial stress periods, suggesting climate risks amplify systemic vulnerabilities.
key_points: Banks with higher financed emissions (carbon-intensive borrower exposure) face 7-12% higher repo rates per standard deviation increase in transition risk
The premium combines a risk premium (compensating for credit risk) and an "inconvenience premium" (dealer banks' sustainability preferences)
The carbon premium triples in size during periods of market stress, amplifying systemic risk
Repo rates for high-emission banks adjust more quickly to ECB rate hikes, indicating uneven monetary policy pass-through
Data covers EUR 500-1,500 billion daily repo volumes from 46 MMSR-reporting banks and 223 borrowing counterparties
Analysis covers 2019-2022 period using ECB's Money Market Statistical Reporting dataset
why_it_matters: Climate transition risk is already being priced into bank funding costs in the core European repo market, with implications for financial stability and monetary policy transmission. Banks with carbon-intensive loan portfolios face higher short-term funding costs, creating potential pro-cyclical pressures if climate policies tighten. The uneven pass-through of rate changes across banks complicates ECB policy transmission.
macro_context: Transition risk is identified as more near-term material than physical risk for financial institutions. The paper contributes to understanding how climate risks affect systemic risk and monetary policy transmission channels.
market_structure_context: The paper directly examines repo market structure—the primary venue for European bank funding and ECB policy rate transmission. It identifies transition risk as a new segmentation factor in interbank lending markets, with climate-committed dealers charging premium rates to carbon-exposed borrowers. This adds a new dimension to repo pricing alongside collateral quality, maturity, and counterparty relationships.
important_numbers: 7-12% repo rate increase per standard deviation in financed emissions
3x carbon premium amplification during market stress
